did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework named DermSense for skin lesion classification. It integrates the CLIP vision-language model, Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate fine-grained attributes, visual prototypes, and a cross-modal fusion module with multi-head self-attention. The method was evaluated on dermatological datasets (ISIC2018 and Derm7pt) to classify skin lesions, including melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel framework named 'DermSense' for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodological contributions (visual prototypes, LLM-generated attributes, cross-modal fusion), describes implementation details (datasets ISIC2018 and Derm7pt, hyperparameters, training time), and reports quantitative results including balanced accuracy metrics and ablation studies. It is published in the 'Proceedings of SEML 2025 Symposium', indicating it is a conference paper presenting new technical results rather than a review.

title: Injecting Visual Prototypes into CLIP-driven Long-Tailed Skin Lesion Classification
journal: Proceedings of SEML 2025 Symposium: Machine Learning Theory and Applications
brief_summary: This study proposes DermSense, a novel framework for skin lesion classification that addresses long-tailed data distributions and insufficient visual information utilization. The method integrates Large Language Model (LLM)-generated fine-grained attributes and visual prototypes with the CLIP vision-language model, utilizing a cross-modal fusion module and a Logits-Adjusted loss function. The approach was evaluated on the ISIC2018 and Derm7pt datasets, focusing on balanced accuracy and melanoma classification. Results demonstrate that DermSense significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like MONET and CBI-VLM in balanced accuracy, particularly for melanoma detection. Additionally, the framework achieves substantial computational efficiency, requiring only 4–13 minutes of training time compared to hours for existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; explicitly highlighted for superior classification performance and inference focus due to high mortality rate. Included in both ISIC2018 and Derm7pt datasets.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. Filtered to include only nevus and melanoma lesions for the Derm7pt dataset.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. Mentioned in introduction as a malignant neoplasm.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. LLM attributes generated for AKIEC (Actinic Keratosis/Intraepithelial Carcinoma) class.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. LLM attributes generated for BKL class.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. LLM attributes generated for DF class.
Included in ISIC2018 dataset. LLM attributes generated for VASC class.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin lesion classification' as stated in the title and abstract. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a Logits-Adjusted loss function and evaluates performance using Balanced Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity on the ISIC2018 (7 classes) and Derm7pt (2 classes: nevus and melanoma) datasets. The inference specifically targets determining whether an image represents a melanoma lesion.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic dataset containing images of various skin lesions, also known as the HAM10000 dataset. It includes multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the DermSense framework and baselines (CLIP, MONET, CBI-VLM) with a Logits-Adjusted loss function to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating balanced accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
use.other: Used for cross-dataset evaluation where models trained on ISIC2018 were tested on Derm7pt.

datasets_used.name: Derm7pt
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising both clinical and dermoscopic images. The study filtered this dataset to include only nevus and melanoma lesions.

use.training: Used for training the DermSense framework and baselines.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating balanced accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
use.other: Used as the target dataset in cross-dataset evaluation experiments.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To mitigate class imbalance in long-tailed data distributions by calibrating predicted logits with class prior probabilities during training.
To evaluate and compare classification performance across imbalanced datasets (ISIC2018 and Derm7pt).

statistical_methods_used.method: Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) and Specificity (True Negative Rate)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance, particularly for melanoma detection, and assess the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (Balanced Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018.description: Dataset with 7 classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions). Splits: Train 8012, Validation 2003, Test 1511.
ISIC2018.notes: ViT-B/32 attained the highest balanced accuracy among baselines and early DermSense variants on this dataset. DermSense models significantly reduced training time (4-13 min) compared to baselines (5h).

Derm7pt.description: Dataset filtered to 2 classes (melanocytic nevus, melanoma). Splits: Train 346, Validation 161, Test 320.
Derm7pt.notes: ViT-B/16 achieved the best BAcc (75.6%) and specificity (94.5%) on this dataset. CBI-VLM demonstrated high sensitivity (58.8%).

Cross-Dataset Evaluation (ISIC2018 -> De.description: Models trained on ISIC2018 and evaluated on Derm7pt.
Cross-Dataset Evaluation (ISIC2018 -> De.notes: DermSense (ViT-B/16) outperformed alternatives in BAcc, sensitivity, and specificity, demonstrating better generalization across datasets.

Ablation Study (ISIC2018).description: Evaluation of individual components of DermSense using ViT-B/16 backbone on ISIC2018.
Ablation Study (ISIC2018).notes: The full combination of all components achieved the highest BAcc of 72.1%. Cross-modal fusion played a critical role in performance improvement.

architecture_used.main_model: DermSense (a framework integrating CLIP with LLM-generated attributes and visual prototypes)
architecture_used.pretraining: CLIP backbone pre-trained on 400 million image-text pairs; LLM (GPT-4o) used to generate fine-grained attributes; DeepLabv3 fine-tuned for segmentation
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images segmented via DeepLabv3)

architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of image features, visual prototypes, and textual features followed by multi-head self-attention

training_details.loss_function: Logits-Adjusted (LA) loss applied to logits from visual and textual modalities

classification_or_output_head.details: Ensemble of visual prototype logits and textual feature logits; final logits z = z_V + z_T

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Melbin et al. (SVM with ABCD features)
Zanddizari et al. (CNN with ROI preprocessing)
preprocessing_techniques: Lesion segmentation using DeepLabv3 to isolate lesion areas from background
Filtering Derm7pt dataset to include only nevus and melanoma lesions
Use of off-the-shelf segmentation model to process images before feature extraction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a training loss function to address class imbalance in long-tailed data distributions by calibrating predicted logits with class prior probabilities (p(y=i) estimated as n_i / sum(n_k)). This acts as a form of class balancing augmentation during optimization rather than input data transformation.

conclusions: The proposed DermSense framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines (CLIP, MONET, CBI-VLM) in balanced accuracy on the ISIC2018 and Derm7pt datasets, particularly for melanoma classification.
DermSense achieves superior computational efficiency, requiring only 4–13 minutes of training time compared to approximately 5 hours for baseline methods, while maintaining high performance.

The integration of LLM-generated fine-grained attributes and visual prototypes effectively addresses the challenges of long-tailed data distributions and insufficient visual information utilization in skin lesion classification.

The cross-modal fusion module plays a critical role in performance improvement, with the full model achieving the highest balanced accuracy (72.1% on ISIC2018) when combining attributes, Logits-Adjusted loss, visual prototypes, and fusion.
DermSense demonstrates strong generalization capabilities in cross-dataset evaluation (ISIC2018 to Derm7pt), outperforming baselines in balanced accuracy (33.7%), sensitivity, and specificity.

The Logits-Adjusted loss function effectively mitigates class imbalance by calibrating predicted logits with class prior probabilities, ensuring balanced learning across categories without distorting the original data distribution.
The method maintains scalability and efficiency by leveraging CLIP's pre-trained capabilities without fine-tuning the backbone network, making it suitable for clinical deployment constraints.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that clinical applicability has not been extensively validated, specifically noting the need for diverse datasets encompassing a broader range of skin tones and disease types.
The framework's integration into clinical practice requires further exploration to ensure it meets requirements for transparency, explainability, and practicality in real-world scenarios.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values); performance comparisons rely on descriptive statistics (Balanced Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity).
The model relies on GPT-4o to generate fine-grained attributes, introducing a dependency on an external proprietary LLM which may affect reproducibility and cost.
The study uses an off-the-shelf segmentation model (DeepLabv3) for preprocessing, which may introduce segmentation errors that propagate to the classification stage.

Cross-dataset evaluation (ISIC2018 to Derm7pt) shows significantly lower performance (BAcc 33.7%) compared to in-dataset evaluation (BAcc 75.6%), indicating potential generalization limitations across different data distributions.
The Derm7pt dataset was filtered to include only nevus and melanoma lesions, limiting the generalizability of results for other skin lesion types within that specific dataset context.
